title: How does HashSet ensure uniqueness of its elements?

answers.junior: HashSet ensures uniqueness by internally delegating to a HashMap, where each element is stored as a key with a dummy constant value (PRESENT). When you call add(element), HashSet calls hashCode() on the element to determine the bucket location, then uses equals() to check for an existing element in that bucket.
answers.middle: HashSet ensures uniqueness by internally delegating to a HashMap, where each element is stored as a key with a dummy constant value (PRESENT). When you call add(element), HashSet calls hashCode() on the element to determine the bucket location, then uses equals() to check for an existing element in that bucket. If an element with the same hash and equal content already exists, the add operation returns false and no duplicate is inserted. This means every object stored in a HashSet must have consistent and correct implementations of both hashCode() and equals() — if two objects are equal via equals(), they must return the same hashCode(), as required by the general contract defined in Object. A common production pitfall is using mutable objects as HashSet elements: if an object's state changes after insertion (altering its hashCode), the set can no longer locate it, effectively creating a "lost" element and breaking the uniqueness guarantee.
answers.senior: HashSet ensures uniqueness by internally delegating to a HashMap, where each element is stored as a key with a dummy constant value (PRESENT). When you call add(element), HashSet calls hashCode() on the element to determine the bucket location, then uses equals() to check for an existing element in that bucket. If an element with the same hash and equal content already exists, the add operation returns false and no duplicate is inserted. This means every object stored in a HashSet must have consistent and correct implementations of both hashCode() and equals() — if two objects are equal via equals(), they must return the same hashCode(), as required by the general contract defined in Object. A common production pitfall is using mutable objects as HashSet elements: if an object's state changes after insertion (altering its hashCode), the set can no longer locate it, effectively creating a "lost" element and breaking the uniqueness guarantee. For example, storing a custom Person object that overrides equals() and hashCode() based on name and age works correctly only if those fields are not mutated after insertion. The initial capacity (default 16) and load factor (default 0.75) influence when the underlying HashMap resizes, which rehashes all entries and can be expensive for large sets. For thread-safe scenarios, Collections.synchronizedSet(new HashSet<>()) or ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet() (Java 8+) should be used instead of bare HashSet. The time complexity for add, remove, and contains is O(1) amortized, degrading to O(n) in pathological cases of hash collisions, which is why a well-distributed hashCode implementation is critical for performance.
answers.tricky: HashSet ensures uniqueness by internally delegating to a HashMap, where each element is stored as a key with a dummy constant value (PRESENT). When you call add(element), HashSet calls hashCode() on the element to determine the bucket location, then uses equals() to check for an existing element in that bucket. If an element with the same hash and equal content already exists, the add operation returns false and no duplicate is inserted. This means every object stored in a HashSet must have consistent and correct implementations of both hashCode() and equals() — if two objects are equal via equals(), they must return the same hashCode(), as required by the general contract defined in Object. A common production pitfall is using mutable objects as HashSet elements: if an object's state changes after insertion (altering its hashCode), the set can no longer locate it, effectively creating a "lost" element and breaking the uniqueness guarantee. For example, storing a custom Person object that overrides equals() and hashCode() based on name and age works correctly only if those fields are not mutated after insertion. The initial capacity (default 16) and load factor (default 0.75) influence when the underlying HashMap resizes, which rehashes all entries and can be expensive for large sets. For thread-safe scenarios, Collections.synchronizedSet(new HashSet<>()) or ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet() (Java 8+) should be used instead of bare HashSet. The time complexity for add, remove, and contains is O(1) amortized, degrading to O(n) in pathological cases of hash collisions, which is why a well-distributed hashCode implementation is critical for performance.
